Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd usually wait until I felt like I absolutely had to do something, then I'd binge through materials for a few hours. Being 25 and having limited resources, I often had to work around other responsibilities, so studying happened whenever I could squeeze it in. I tried to make it visual—lots of diagrams and color-coded notes—but the consistency just wasn't there. Most weeks I'd manage maybe 12 hours, but it felt chaotic rather than planned.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due. I'd have these quiet weeks where I barely touched anything, then suddenly a deadline would loom and I'd be cramming everything in at once. Regular times weren't really my thing—life just got in the way too often. Sometimes I'd plan to study on certain evenings, but then something would come up or I'd just feel too drained to follow through.
Q3: Usually it was panic about a deadline that got me to log in. I'd realize something was due soon and suddenly the LMS became urgent. Sometimes I'd get a notification or remember during a conversation with a classmate that I needed to check something. It wasn't really a habit—more like a reaction to pressure or fear of missing something important.
Q4: There were brief periods where logging in felt more natural, especially after the peer support intervention connected me with some classmates. Chatting with them made me want to check in more regularly to see what they were discussing. But honestly, that routine never fully stuck. It would last maybe a week or two before I'd drift back to my old sporadic patterns.
Q5: The video lectures were the most useful for me because I'm a visual learner. I could pause, rewind, and really absorb the diagrams and demonstrations. I watched about 96 minutes worth, which doesn't sound like much, but I'd often replay sections multiple times. The discussion posts were helpful too when classmates explained things in different ways than the instructor.
Q6: I ignored a lot of the reading materials and some of the supplementary resources. Dense text just doesn't work for my brain—I'd start reading and zone out within minutes. The forum discussions I participated in sometimes, but I'd skip the ones that seemed too theoretical or where people were just showing off. If something didn't immediately seem relevant to passing, I probably skipped it.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to the assignments or exams. If I watched a video and immediately understood how it would help me complete something, that felt valuable. Also, anything that helped me connect with other students felt worthwhile because those interactions actually lifted my mood. Visual content that I could reference later always seemed like a good investment.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford, honestly. With limited time and energy, I had to prioritize what was required just to pass. Required activities had this weight to them—skip them and you fail. Optional stuff, even if interesting, always got pushed to the bottom of my list. I think I would have engaged more with optional content if I'd had more breathing room in my life.
Q9: Yeah, definitely. I'd download lecture slides and videos to my laptop so I could study without needing internet sometimes. My connection isn't always reliable, and data costs money I don't have. I also used YouTube videos on similar topics when the course materials weren't clicking. Took handwritten notes too—something about writing things out helps me remember better than typing.
Q10: There were many times I'd be studying from printed notes or downloaded slides for hours, but the LMS would show nothing. I'd sit at the library with my notebook, working through problems, and none of that registered anywhere. Sometimes I'd discuss concepts with my peer support group in person, which was real learning, but completely invisible to any tracking system.
Q11: All the time. I'd watch a video once online, then download the slides and work from those for days. Or I'd access a reading, save it as a PDF, and annotate it on my tablet offline. The LMS would just show one quick access, but I might have spent hours with that material afterward. It was more practical for me to work offline given my situation.
Q12: There were definitely low points, especially mid-semester when everything piled up at once. I was dealing with some financial stress and feeling pretty sad about where I was in life at 25. The motivation was there in theory—I knew I needed this—but actually sitting down and engaging felt impossible some days. My attendance dropped because I just couldn't face it.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted from working or stressed about money, the LMS became this thing I'd avoid. I'd log in, see all the tasks, feel overwhelmed, and log right back out. Competing responsibilities meant coursework often came last. The fatigue made it hard to concentrate even when I did engage, so I'd end up just going through the motions without really absorbing anything.
Q14: More often than I'd like to admit. I'd have the videos playing but my mind would be elsewhere—worrying about bills or just feeling down. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them. Sometimes I'd click through materials just to feel like I was doing something, but I wasn't actually learning. It was engagement theater, I guess.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, even when I knew they mattered. Part of it was avoidance—if something seemed hard or confusing, I'd convince myself I'd do it later when I felt more capable. But that capable feeling rarely came. The anxiety of starting something difficult was worse than the anxiety of having it hanging over me, weirdly enough. I'd wait until the pressure became unbearable.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. Without them, I'd drift aimlessly through the course. When a deadline approached, suddenly I'd be logging in constantly, watching videos at 1.5x speed, frantically completing assignments. My LMS activity probably looked like flatlines punctuated by spikes of panic. It wasn't healthy, but it's how I survived.
Q17: When I got feedback, especially positive feedback on assignments, it did give me a temporary boost. Seeing that I scored well made me think maybe I could actually do this. But negative or critical feedback sometimes made me want to disengage more—it confirmed this voice in my head saying I wasn't good enough. The peer support helped balance that out somewhat.
Q18: The course structure was okay, but having 12 online courses to juggle made everything feel fragmented. I couldn't give proper attention to any single course. The weekly modules helped me know what was expected, but the flexibility also meant I could easily fall behind without immediate consequences. More structure might have actually helped someone like me who tends to drift.
Q19: The video content made engagement easier because it suited my learning style. But the sheer volume of reading materials made it harder—I'd see walls of text and immediately feel defeated. The discussion requirements were good because they forced some interaction, but sometimes the prompts felt disconnected from what actually mattered for assessments.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is anxious and keeps checking things without actually completing work. Like, you're logging in repeatedly because you're worried, watching videos to feel productive, but the actual assignments feel too daunting to submit. Or maybe they're trying to understand material but struggling, so they engage a lot without producing results. I've been close to that pattern myself.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat. My LMS activity was moderate at best, but I managed to pass with decent assignment scores. A lot of my actual learning happened offline—with downloaded materials, handwritten notes, and conversations with peers. Someone looking at my login frequency might think I wasn't trying, but I was working in ways the system couldn't see.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when someone logs in, what they click on, how long they spend. That's useful for spotting someone who's completely disappeared. But it misses everything that happens offline, the quality of attention during online sessions, and the emotional state behind the numbers. It can't tell if someone watched a video while crying or while deeply focused.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling connected to the process. It's not just completing tasks—it's wanting to understand, being curious, feeling like the material matters to your life somehow. For me, engagement also means feeling connected to other people in the course, not just isolated with content. The peer support helped me feel more engaged than I had been.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much the peer support intervention actually meant to me, or how my mood shifted because of those connections. It wouldn't capture the sadness I carried into most study sessions, or how hard I was fighting just to show up at all. It also wouldn't show the offline studying, the financial stress affecting my concentration, or the fact that passing this course at 25 feels like my last chance at something better.
